content.text: Guardrails in the OpenAI Agents SDK
are fast checks that run alongside the main model to enforce safety, policy, and quality constraints without consuming the primary model's context or turn budget. Two types exist:
inspect user messages before the model sees them, and
inspect the model's final response before it reaches the caller.
Guardrails are declared on the agent and run automatically by the
. When a guardrail trips, the SDK raises a typed exception that the application layer can catch and handle gracefully.

attrs.alt: OpenAI Agents SDK guardrail execution flow diagram

content.text: Input and output guardrails both follow the same execution contract:
Each guardrail is an async function that receives the run context, the active agent, and the input or output being checked.
Multiple guardrails registered on the same agent run concurrently.

Guardrails run concurrently with the main model call (input guardrails) or immediately after it (output guardrails). They are intended to be fast — use a small model or a regex, not a large LLM — so they do not add significant latency.
or by passing a class instance that inherits from
. The guardrail receives the raw user input before it is sent to the main model.
A more flexible pattern uses a small, fast model as the guardrail to handle ambiguous cases.

from pydantic import BaseModel
from agents import Agent, Runner, input_guardrail, GuardrailFunctionOutput, RunContextWrapper

class ClassificationResult(BaseModel):
    is_safe: bool
    reason: str

guardrail_model = Agent(
    name="SafetyClassifier",
    instructions=(
        "Classify the input as safe (is_safe=true) or unsafe (is_safe=false). "
        "Unsafe means hate speech, self-harm instructions, or illegal activity."
    ),
    output_type=ClassificationResult,
    model="gpt-4o-mini",   # fast, cheap model for guardrail
)

@input_guardrail
async def llm_safety_check(
    ctx: RunContextWrapper,
    agent: Agent,
    input,
) -> GuardrailFunctionOutput:
    result = await Runner.run(guardrail_model, input, context=ctx.context)
    classification: ClassificationResult = result.final_output
    return GuardrailFunctionOutput(
        output_info=classification.model_dump(),
        tripwire_triggered=not classification.is_safe,
    )

main_agent = Agent(
    name="MainAgent",
    instructions="Answer questions helpfully.",
    input_guardrails=[llm_safety_check],
)
Output guardrails inspect the final response produced by the main agent. They receive the agent's output (a string or a structured Pydantic object if
When a guardrail trips, the SDK raises a typed exception. Catch it in your application layer to return a safe response to the user.

from agents import (
    Agent, Runner,
    InputGuardrailTripwireTriggered,
    OutputGuardrailTripwireTriggered,
)

agent = Agent(
    name="SafeAgent",
    instructions="Answer questions.",
    input_guardrails=[block_profanity],
    output_guardrails=[no_pii_in_output],
)

async def handle_message(user_message: str) -> str:
    try:
        result = await Runner.run(agent, user_message)
        return result.final_output
    except InputGuardrailTripwireTriggered as e:
        info = e.guardrail_result.output.output_info
        print(f"Input blocked: {info}")
        return "I'm unable to respond to that request."
    except OutputGuardrailTripwireTriggered as e:
        info = e.guardrail_result.output.output_info
        print(f"Output blocked: {info}")
        return "My response was withheld. Please contact support."
True stops the run and raises an exception
Arbitrary data attached to the exception for logging; not shown to the user
. This lets you log the specific guardrail that fired, the reason, and any diagnostic data without exposing it to the end user.
Register as many guardrails as needed. They all run concurrently. The run stops as soon as any one of them sets

from agents import Agent

agent = Agent(
    name="ProductionBot",
    instructions="Answer customer questions.",
    input_guardrails=[
        block_profanity,
        llm_safety_check,
        check_rate_limit,        # custom: throttle by user_id in context
    ],
    output_guardrails=[
        no_pii_in_output,
        no_competitor_mentions,  # custom: brand safety
    ],
)
All input guardrails start concurrently when the run begins. Because they run in parallel, registering five fast guardrails costs roughly the same wall-clock time as registering one.
Guardrails run on every message. A slow guardrail directly increases response latency. Target under 200 ms for regex-based checks and under 1 second for model-based checks using a small, fast model.
when tripping the guardrail. Without it, the exception carries no context — debugging becomes guesswork.
The SDK may retry a turn. Your guardrail may be called more than once with the same input. Avoid side effects that shouldn't repeat (e.g. charging a rate-limit credit on every call).

Guardrails are safety nets, not business logic routers. If you find yourself writing complex routing or transformation logic inside a guardrail, move that logic into a proper agent or explicit chaining step instead.

import asyncio
import pytest
from agents import Agent, Runner, InputGuardrailTripwireTriggered

agent = Agent(
    name="SafeBot",
    instructions="Answer questions.",
    input_guardrails=[block_profanity],
)

def test_clean_input_passes():
    result = Runner.run_sync(agent, "What is the weather today?")
    assert result.final_output  # run completed normally

def test_profanity_is_blocked():
    with pytest.raises(InputGuardrailTripwireTriggered) as exc_info:
        Runner.run_sync(agent, "badword1 in my question")
    info = exc_info.value.guardrail_result.output.output_info
    assert info["reason"] == "profanity_detected"
For more complex guardrails that need constructor arguments or shared state, subclass

Guardrails are declared per agent. In a multi-agent system with handoffs, each agent runs only its own guardrails. If you want a guardrail that fires regardless of which agent handles the request, register it on every agent in the chain.

# Global guardrail applied to all agents in a system
global_input_guardrails = [block_profanity, llm_safety_check]

triage = Agent(
    name="Triage",
    instructions="Route to the right specialist.",
    input_guardrails=global_input_guardrails,
    handoffs=[billing, tech],
)

billing = Agent(
    name="Billing",
    instructions="Handle billing questions.",
    input_guardrails=global_input_guardrails,  # also applied here
)

tech = Agent(
    name="Tech",
    instructions="Handle technical questions.",
    input_guardrails=global_input_guardrails,  # and here
)
Output guardrails only fire for the agent that produces the
response. In a handoff chain, only the last active agent's output guardrails run. If you need to check all intermediate outputs, use step callbacks instead.
Guardrails and step callbacks serve different purposes. Knowing which to use prevents over-engineering.
After every model call and tool call
Input guardrails that have access to the run context can implement per-user rate limiting by reading a token bucket from the context object.
A production agent typically layers multiple complementary guardrails. Here is a complete example that composes three input guardrails and two output guardrails:

from agents import Agent

production_agent = Agent(
    name="ProductionAssistant",
    instructions="Answer customer support questions for Acme Inc.",
    model="gpt-4o",
    input_guardrails=[
        block_profanity,          # cheap regex: < 1 ms
        rate_limit,               # counter lookup: < 1 ms
        llm_safety_check,         # small model: ~300 ms, runs in parallel
    ],
    output_guardrails=[
        no_pii_in_output,         # regex PII scan
        no_competitor_mentions,   # brand safety check
    ],
)

# All three input guardrails fire concurrently.
# If the safety check is slow, it does not delay the profanity and rate-limit checks.
# The run stops as soon as the first guardrail trips.
Input guardrails run concurrently before the main model call; output guardrails run after the final response is produced
— catch these at the application boundary
Multiple guardrails on the same agent run in parallel — first to trip stops the run
Use a small, fast model (e.g. gpt-4o-mini) for model-based guardrails to keep latency low
so exceptions carry enough context for logging and debugging
Test guardrails in isolation — they are plain async functions and easy to unit test without a real LLM
